From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Beverage made from infusing or decocting plant material in hot water

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/Hibiscus_Delight_tisane.jpg/250px-Hibiscus_Delight_tisane.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hibiscus_Delight_tisane.jpg)

"Hibiscus Delight", made from hibiscus flowers, rose hips, orange peel, green tea, and red raspberry leaf,[[1]](#cite_note-1) [steeping](/wiki/Steeping "Steeping")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/Butterfly-pea_flower_tea_3.jpg/250px-Butterfly-pea_flower_tea_3.jpg)](/wiki/File:Butterfly-pea_flower_tea_3.jpg)

[Butterfly-pea flower tea](/wiki/Butterfly_pea_flower_tea "Butterfly pea flower tea"). The one on the right has had lime juice added, making it turn [purple](/wiki/Purple "Purple").

**Herbal teas** or **herb teas,** technically known as **herbal infusions**, and less commonly[[2]](#cite_note-ngram-2) called **tisanes**,[[3]](#cite_note-cam-3) are beverages made from the [infusion](/wiki/Infusion "Infusion") or [decoction](/wiki/Decoction "Decoction") of any [herbs](/wiki/Herb "Herb"), [spices](/wiki/Spice "Spice"), or other [plant](/wiki/Plant "Plant") material in water that is not derived from the tea plant (*[Camellia sinensis](/wiki/Camellia_sinensis "Camellia sinensis")*). Many herbs used in teas/tisanes are also used in [herbal medicine](/wiki/Herbal_medicine "Herbal medicine") and in [folk medicine](/wiki/Traditional_medicine "Traditional medicine"). Herbal teas are not technically teas because they are not brewed from the [tea](/wiki/Tea "Tea") plant.

The term "herbal tea" is often used to distinguish these infusions from true teas (e.g., [black](/wiki/Black_tea "Black tea"), [green](/wiki/Green_tea "Green tea"), [white](/wiki/White_tea "White tea"), [yellow](/wiki/Yellow_tea "Yellow tea"), [oolong](/wiki/Oolong "Oolong")), which are prepared from the tea plant (*Camellia sinensis*) or, more rarely, its close relatives (such as *[Camellia taliensis](/wiki/Camellia_taliensis "Camellia taliensis")*)*.* Beverages actually created from tea and combined with material from other plants are known as "blended teas", "scented teas", or "flavored teas". Examples include [jasmine tea](/wiki/Jasmine_tea "Jasmine tea"), [genmaicha](/wiki/Genmaicha "Genmaicha"), and [Earl Grey tea](/wiki/Earl_Grey_tea "Earl Grey tea"). Unlike true teas, most tisanes do not naturally contain [caffeine](/wiki/Caffeine "Caffeine") (though tea can be [decaffeinated](/wiki/Decaffeinated "Decaffeinated"), i.e., processed to remove caffeine).[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

A number of plants, however, *do* contain psychoactive compounds, such as caffeine or another [stimulant](/wiki/Stimulant "Stimulant"), like [theobromine](/wiki/Theobromine "Theobromine"), [cocaine](/wiki/Cocaine "Cocaine") or [ephedrine](/wiki/Ephedrine "Ephedrine"). Some have the opposite effect, acting as a [sedative](/wiki/Sedative "Sedative"). Some common [infusions](/wiki/Infusion "Infusion") have specific names such as [*mate*](/wiki/Mate_(drink) "Mate (drink)") (yerba mate) and *[rooibos](/wiki/Rooibos "Rooibos")* (red bush).

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Herbal_tea&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/Paul_Berthon_-_Tisane_Gauloise.jpg/250px-Paul_Berthon_-_Tisane_Gauloise.jpg)](/wiki/File:Paul_Berthon_-_Tisane_Gauloise.jpg)

A promotional french poster for "Tisane Gauloise", by [Paul Berthon](/wiki/Paul_Berthon "Paul Berthon")

Dictionaries commonly record that the word *tea* is used to refer to other plants beside the [tea plant](/wiki/Camellia_sinensis "Camellia sinensis") and to beverages made from these other plants.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) The term *herbal tea* is well established and more common than *tisane* for this usage[[2]](#cite_note-ngram-2)

Furthermore, in the *[Etymology of tea](/wiki/Etymology_of_tea "Etymology of tea"),* the most ancient term for tea was [荼](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E8%8D%BC "wiktionary:荼") (pronounced *tu*) which originally referred to various plants such as [sow thistle](/wiki/Sow_thistle "Sow thistle"), [chicory](/wiki/Chicory "Chicory"), or [smartweed](/wiki/Polygonum "Polygonum"), and was later used to exclusively refer to *[Camellia sinensis](/wiki/Camellia_sinensis "Camellia sinensis")* (true "tea").[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)

The word *tisane* was rare in its modern sense before the 20th century, when it was borrowed in the modern sense from French. This is why some people feel it should be pronounced [/tɪˈzɑːn/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") as in [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"), but the original English pronunciation [/tɪˈzæn/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") continues to be more common in US English and especially in UK English.[[3]](#cite_note-cam-3)

The word had already existed in late [Middle English](/wiki/Middle_English "Middle English") in the sense of "medicinal drink" and had already been borrowed from French ([Old French](/wiki/Old_French "Old French")). The Old French word came from the Latin word *ptisana*, which came from the [Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek") word πτισάνη (*ptisánē*), which meant "peeled" barley, in other words [pearl barley](/wiki/Pearl_barley "Pearl barley"), and a drink made from this that is similar to modern [barley water](/wiki/Barley_water "Barley water").[[10]](#cite_note-10)

## Composition and usage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Herbal_tea&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Composition and usage")]

See also: [List of plants used in herbalism](/wiki/List_of_plants_used_in_herbalism "List of plants used in herbalism") and [List of culinary herbs and spices](/wiki/List_of_culinary_herbs_and_spices "List of culinary herbs and spices")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/LE_POINT_DE_VUE11n4000.jpg/250px-LE_POINT_DE_VUE11n4000.jpg)](/wiki/File:LE_POINT_DE_VUE11n4000.jpg)

Herbal tea in a glass [teapot](/wiki/Teapot "Teapot") and [cup](/wiki/Teacup "Teacup")

Herbal teas can be made with any part of a plant, including fresh or dried [flowers](/wiki/Flower "Flower"), [fruit](/wiki/Fruit "Fruit"), [leaves](/wiki/Leaf "Leaf"), stems, seeds, or [roots](/wiki/Root "Root"). These parts may be steeped fully raw (as picked) or processed in some way (such as drying, roasting, crushing, tearing/cutting, steaming, etc.).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/Herbal_tisanes_for_several_ailments_on_sale_at_the_Central_Market%2C_Port_Louis%2C_Mauritius.jpg/250px-Herbal_tisanes_for_several_ailments_on_sale_at_the_Central_Market%2C_Port_Louis%2C_Mauritius.jpg)](/wiki/File:Herbal_tisanes_for_several_ailments_on_sale_at_the_Central_Market,_Port_Louis,_Mauritius.jpg)

Herbal tisanes being sold in [Mauritius](/wiki/Mauritius "Mauritius") as medicines for various diseases

Herbal infusions may be made by pouring hot or [boiling](/wiki/Boiling "Boiling") water over the plant parts and letting them steep for some time. The infusion temperature and time can vary depending on the type of plant part used and its properties. For example, some plant parts are covered in oils, which may take some time to separate. [Brewing with cold water](/wiki/Cold_brew_tea "Cold brew tea") will also take much longer, usually several hours.

An herbal tea may be strained or not (as with [mate](/wiki/Mate_(drink) "Mate (drink)"), where a special straw called a [bombilla](/wiki/Bombilla "Bombilla") is used for drinking).[[11]](#cite_note-Heiss2012-11)

Some herbal teas are blends that include various herbs or plant parts.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Herbal infusions may also be sweetened, spiced, salted, or combined with other additives, like milk or lemon juice.

Herbal teas can be made from any edible plant material, below is a list of common herbal infusions. Some herbal teas are made from plants which contain [caffeine](/wiki/Caffeine "Caffeine"), and other herbal infusions may contain other [psychoactive](/wiki/Psychoactive_drug "Psychoactive drug") compounds. However, many other common herbal teas have not been shown psychoactive properties when compared to [placebos](/wiki/Placebo "Placebo"), though they may still have some physical effects.

Many herbal teas on the market may also be blends which include various herbs or plant parts. These blends may also include additives, like [flavorings](/wiki/Flavoring "Flavoring").

### Other psychoactive infusions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Herbal_tea&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Other psychoactive infusions")]

* [Cannabis tea](/wiki/Cannabis_tea "Cannabis tea"), which contains the psychoactive [THC](/wiki/Thc "Thc"). It is a common drink in India, where it is known as [bhang](/wiki/Bhang "Bhang").
* [Coca tea](/wiki/Coca_tea "Coca tea"), infusion made from [coca](/wiki/Coca "Coca") leaves. Contains trace amounts of [cocaine](/wiki/Cocaine "Cocaine") and similar alkaloids.[[12]](#cite_note-ncbi_alkaloids-12) In some countries where coca is illegal, products marketed as "coca tea" are supposed to be *decocainized*, i.e., the pharmacologically active components have been removed from the leaf using the same chemicals as manufacturing cocaine.
* [Kava](/wiki/Kava "Kava") root tea, common in Pacific island cultures ([Polynesia](/wiki/Polynesia "Polynesia"), [Melanesia](/wiki/Melanesia "Melanesia"), [Micronesia](/wiki/Micronesia "Micronesia")), which has [sedative effects](/wiki/Sedation "Sedation") and [anesthetic](/wiki/Anesthetic "Anesthetic") effects caused by compounds called [kavalactones](/wiki/Kavalactone "Kavalactone").[[13]](#cite_note-wang-13) The traditional form is a water-based suspension of kava roots.
* [Kratom](/wiki/Kratom "Kratom") tea made from the dried leaves of the kratom tree. It has [opioid](/wiki/Opioid "Opioid")-like properties and some [stimulant](/wiki/Stimulant "Stimulant")-like effects.[[14]](#cite_note-FDA2018-14)[[15]](#cite_note-Cin2015-15)
* [St. John's wort](/wiki/St._John%27s_wort "St. John's wort") tea, the plant has been shown to have [antidepressant](/wiki/Antidepressant "Antidepressant") properties according to a 2017 [meta-analysis](/wiki/Meta-analysis "Meta-analysis").[[16]](#cite_note-16)
* [Ephedra](/wiki/Ephedra_(plant) "Ephedra (plant)") tea, mainly from the plant *[Ephedra sinica](/wiki/Ephedra_sinica "Ephedra sinica")*.[[17]](#cite_note-Gurley_1998-17) It contains the stimulant [ephedrine](/wiki/Ephedrine "Ephedrine"). It has been used in [traditional Chinese medicine](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_medicine "Traditional Chinese medicine"), where it is called *máhuáng,* for more than 2,000 years.[[18]](#cite_note-Abourashed_2003-18)[[19]](#cite_note-Huang2010-19) Ephedra tea was also used by [Native Americans](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States") and [Mormons](/wiki/Mormon_pioneers "Mormon pioneers"), hence the name "Mormon tea" and "Indian tea".
* [Poppy tea](/wiki/Poppy_tea "Poppy tea"), brewed from the [poppy straw](/wiki/Poppy_straw "Poppy straw") or [seeds](/wiki/Poppy_seed "Poppy seed") of several species of [poppy](/wiki/Poppy "Poppy") (most commonly *[Papaver somniferum](/wiki/Papaver_somniferum "Papaver somniferum")*). Since it contains [opium](/wiki/Opium "Opium"), it is consumed for its [narcotic](/wiki/Narcotic "Narcotic"), sedative and [analgesic](/wiki/Analgesic "Analgesic") properties.
* *[Salvia divinorum](/wiki/Salvia_divinorum "Salvia divinorum")* ("Sage of the diviners") tea which contains *[salvinorin A](/wiki/Salvinorin_A "Salvinorin A")*, a compound that induces a [dissociative state](/wiki/Dissociation_(psychology) "Dissociation (psychology)") and [hallucinations](/wiki/Hallucination "Hallucination").[[20]](#cite_note-front-20)
* [African dream root](/wiki/Silene_undulata "Silene undulata") *(Silene undulata)* which is an oneirogen used by the [Xhosa people](/wiki/Xhosa_people "Xhosa people") as a [sacred plant](/wiki/Entheogen "Entheogen").[[21]](#cite_note-Sobiecki-21)[[22]](#cite_note-pmid29403350-22)
* [Ayahuasca](/wiki/Ayahuasca "Ayahuasca"), a highly psychoactive and [hallucinogenic](/wiki/Hallucinogen "Hallucinogen") infusion brewed from the *[Banisteriopsis caapi](/wiki/Banisteriopsis_caapi "Banisteriopsis caapi")* vine and the leaves of the *[Psychotria viridis](/wiki/Psychotria_viridis "Psychotria viridis")* shrub. It is prepared and drunk by native peoples of the [Amazon basin](/wiki/Amazon_basin "Amazon basin") as a healing drug and as a spiritual [shamanic](/wiki/Shamanism "Shamanism") brew.
* *[Tabernanthe iboga](/wiki/Tabernanthe_iboga "Tabernanthe iboga"),* which can be made into an infusion, is traditionally used by the peoples of [Central Africa](/wiki/Central_Africa "Central Africa") and in [African traditional medicine](/wiki/African_traditional_medicine "African traditional medicine") as a stimulant (in low doses) or as a powerful ritual hallucinogen (in larger quantities)*.[[23]](#cite_note-drugs2-23)[[24]](#cite_note-koenig-24)*[[25]](#cite_note-25)
* [Tobacco](/wiki/Tobacco "Tobacco") tea, which contains [nicotine](/wiki/Nicotine "Nicotine") and is traditionally used by the indigenous peoples of the Americas as a medicine for various ills (dizziness, headaches), as a [laxative](/wiki/Laxative "Laxative"), as an [emetic](/wiki/Vomiting "Vomiting") and as an [expectorant](/wiki/Mucoactive_agent "Mucoactive agent").[[26]](#cite_note-26)

## Health risks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Herbal_tea&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Health risks")]

See also: [List of herbs with known adverse effects](/wiki/List_of_herbs_with_known_adverse_effects "List of herbs with known adverse effects") and [Tea bag § Plastics](/wiki/Tea_bag#Plastics "Tea bag")

While most herbal teas are safe for regular consumption, some herbs have [toxic](/wiki/Toxin "Toxin") or [allergenic](/wiki/Allergy "Allergy") effects. Among the greatest causes of concern are:

* [Comfrey](/wiki/Comfrey "Comfrey"), which contains [alkaloids](/wiki/Alkaloid "Alkaloid") which may be harmful to the [liver](/wiki/Liver "Liver") from chronic use, and particularly is not recommended during pregnancy or when [prescription drugs](/wiki/Prescription_drug "Prescription drug") are used; comfrey is not recommended for oral use.[[33]](#cite_note-33)
* [Lobelia](/wiki/Lobelia "Lobelia"), which contains alkaloids and has [traditional medicine](/wiki/Traditional_medicine "Traditional medicine") uses for smoking cessation, may cause nausea, vomiting, or dizziness at high doses.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

Herbal teas can also have different effects from person to person, and this is further compounded by the problem of potential misidentification. The deadly [foxglove](/wiki/Foxglove "Foxglove"), for example, can be mistaken for the much more benign (but still relatively toxic to the liver) comfrey. Care must be taken not to use any [poisonous plants](/wiki/Poisonous_plants "Poisonous plants").

The US does not require herbal teas to have any evidence concerning their efficacy, but does treat them technically as food products and require that they be safe for consumption.

Fruit or fruit-flavored tea is usually acidic and thus may contribute to erosion of [tooth enamel](/wiki/Tooth_enamel "Tooth enamel").[[35]](#cite_note-O'Toole2018-35)

### Adverse herb–drug interactions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Herbal_tea&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Adverse herb–drug interactions")]

See also: [Grapefruit–drug interactions](/wiki/Grapefruit%E2%80%93drug_interactions "Grapefruit–drug interactions") and [Cytochrome P450 (CYP)](/wiki/Cytochrome_P450 "Cytochrome P450")

Some phytochemicals found in herbs and fruits can adversely interact with others and over the counter or prescription medications, among other ways by affecting their metabolism by the body. Herbs and fruits that inhibit or induce the body's Cytochrome P450 enzyme complex function can either cause the drug to be dangerously ineffective, or increase its effective absorbed dose to potentially toxic levels, respectively. Best known examples of adverse herb‑drug interactions are [grapefruit](/wiki/Grapefruit "Grapefruit") or [St John's wort](/wiki/Hypericum_perforatum "Hypericum perforatum"), contraindicated for several medications including [Paxlovid](/wiki/Paxlovid "Paxlovid") and [oral contraceptives](/wiki/Oral_contraceptives "Oral contraceptives"), but other herbs also affect the CYP enzyme family, showing herb‑drug interactions.[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37)[[38]](#cite_note-38)

### Contamination

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Herbal_tea&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Contamination")]

See also: [Health effects of pesticides](/wiki/Health_effects_of_pesticides "Health effects of pesticides")

Depending on the source of the herbal ingredients, herbal teas, like any crop, may be contaminated with [pesticides](/wiki/Pesticide "Pesticide") or [heavy metals](/wiki/Heavy_metals "Heavy metals").[[39]](#cite_note-NaithaniKakkar2004-39)[[40]](#cite_note-40)
According to Naithani & Kakkar (2004), "all herbal preparations should be checked for toxic chemical residues to allay consumer fears of exposure to known neuro-toxicant pesticides and to aid in promoting global acceptance of these products".[[39]](#cite_note-NaithaniKakkar2004-39)
